article_title: Solar Overtakes Coal in US Electricity for the First Month on Record as May Output Hits 45.5 TWh

content_preview.title: Solar Overtakes Coal in US Electricity for the First Month on Record as May Output Hits 45.5 TWh
content_preview.summary: In May 2026, solar supplied 12.8% of US electricity versus coal's 12.2% — the first month solar has outgenerated coal, according to Ember.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Solar generated more electricity than coal in the United States for the first month on record in May 2026, according to an analysis of official generation data by the energy think tank Ember reported by [Electrek](https://electrek.co/2026/06/09/solar-beats-coal-in-the-us-electricity-mix-for-the-first-time-ever/). Solar supplied a record 12.8% of US electricity for the month, while coal fell to 12.2%.

The crossover marks a symbolic turning point for a US power system in which coal o...

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured News piece (530 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Overview/What We Know/Why May/What We Don't Know structure is logical, and the 'Why May' and 'What We Don't Know' sections add useful seasonal caveats without editorializing. Headline, summary, and lead all accurately reflect the central finding.
editor_notes.source_verification: Both source snapshots read from disk and SHA-256 verified against manifest. source-0.html.gz (Electrek, status 200, hash e322a814... matches): confirmed verbatim — solar 12.8% vs coal 12.2% as first month on record May 2026 per Ember; solar 45.5 TWh record up 17% from May 2025 and above last July's record; solar third-largest US source behind natural gas and nuclear; coal 39.3 TWh April low, 43.4 TWh May, 11% below May 2025; five years ago coal 19.7% / solar 5.4%; March renewables-over-gas US milestone. source-1.html.gz (Utility Dive, status 200, hash a896a289... matches): confirmed solar generation expected to grow 17% over 2025 production this summer and coal to decline about 10% in H1 2026 vs 2025, per EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook published April 6, 2026. Both outlets (electrek.co, utilitydive.com) are on the source allowlist.
editor_notes.ember_handling: The primary Ember report is the origin of the headline finding but is correctly NOT in article.sources (it bot-blocks scrapers with 403). Every Ember-sourced figure is carried verbatim by the allowlisted Electrek snapshot, which itself attributes the analysis to Ember. The article attributes the finding to Ember 'reported by Electrek', which is accurate and preserves provenance. No orphan Ember URL appears in the body — no provenance break.
editor_notes.quote_verification: The Nicolas Fulghum quote is verbatim against the Electrek snapshot. Electrek renders it as two quoted fragments split by the attribution ('US solar power continues to set new records,' said... 'Overtaking coal for the first month on record shows just how far solar has come, from a niche contributor to the third-largest and fastest-growing source of power in the US electricity system.'). The article faithfully joins the two adjacent fragments and truncates before the following sentence ('From Texas to California...'), with no alteration of wording. Attribution to Nicolas Fulghum, senior data analyst at Ember, is correct.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific (12.8%, 12.2%, 45.5 TWh, 17%, 39.3 TWh, 43.4 TWh, 11%, 19.7%, 5.4%, 10%, April 6 2026 STEO) traces verbatim to a cited, allowlisted source. The 'Why May' transition sentence about forecasters projecting other sources to out-generate solar over the full year is editorial framing/context consistent with the EIA outlook (solar remains a growing but not year-leading source); only the precise 17%/10% figures are attributed to Utility Dive, which is correct.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. The single editorial-adjacent phrase ('symbolic turning point') is appropriately hedged and the piece repeatedly stresses seasonality caveats. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: Original. Distinct from the prior 2026-05/27 article (global wind+solar vs gas worldwide, April 2026), which this piece correctly cross-references as a related but separate milestone. No existing article covers the US monthly solar-vs-coal crossover.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. All claims, percentages, the TWh figure, and the Fulghum quote trace verbatim to allowlisted Electrek and Utility Dive snapshots. Ember is correctly excluded from the sources array (403 bot-block) while every Ember figure is carried by Electrek. No provenance break, no duplicate, neutral tone. Ready to publish.
